findings.title: cache_info() raises ValueError on every call due to 5-tuple unpack of 6-tuple cache key
findings.description: The _fetch_cache keys are 6-tuples: (ticker, start, end, interval, source, extended_hours). cache_info() at line 500 unpacks them as 5-tuples. This raises ValueError: too many values to unpack on every invocation of GET /api/cache. The extended_hours field was already in the key before this PR, but the new fetch_ohlcv_async function makes async bot polling the primary code path — meaning /api/cache is more likely to be used for diagnosing dedup behavior. The function was broken before this PR but the PR increases the relevance of the breakage.
findings.evidence: backend/shared.py:428 key = (ticker.upper(), start, end, interval, source, extended_hours) — 6-tuple stored. backend/shared.py:500 for (ticker, start, end, interval, source), (ts, df) in _fetch_cache.items() — 5-tuple unpack raises ValueError immediately.
findings.recommendation: Fix line 500 to unpack as: for (ticker, start, end, interval, source, _ext_hours), (ts, df) in _fetch_cache.items(): and optionally include extended_hours in the key display string.

findings.title: Secondary awaiter of fetch_ohlcv_async receives no error context when deduped future fails
findings.description: When coroutine B deduplicates onto an in-flight future via return await existing (line 484), and that future raises an exception, B re-raises the raw exception to its caller. This is functionally correct. However, B call site in bot_runner.py (line 236) logs Fetch failed: {e} — the message is identical to A log, making it appear as two independent fetches failed rather than one shared fetch. Diagnostic confusion, not a data correctness bug.
findings.evidence: backend/shared.py:482-484 — B takes the existing branch with no exception wrapping. backend/bot_runner.py:236-237 — generic log message. Both bots log the same error at the same time.
findings.recommendation: Low priority. If diagnosability matters, add a distinct log annotation for the deduped-waiter error branch. Not a data correctness issue.

findings.title: Optimizer validation does not guard NaN for parseFloat(min/max) — passes silently if values are non-numeric
findings.description: Lines 104-105 parse p.min and p.max with parseFloat(). If these strings are non-numeric, parseFloat returns NaN. The guard if (minN > maxN) evaluates to false when either operand is NaN (IEEE 754 comparisons with NaN are always false), so a NaN range passes validation silently and reaches linspace() on line 126. linspace(NaN, NaN, N) returns an array of NaN values sent to the backend. In a browser context this is prevented by type=number inputs, but the validation is a correctness gate and is incomplete.
findings.evidence: OptimizerPanel.tsx:104 const minN = p.min !== "" ? parseFloat(p.min) : opt.defaultMin — no isNaN check. Line 107: if (minN > maxN) — NaN comparisons always false, silently passing through.
findings.recommendation: Add if (isNaN(minN) || isNaN(maxN)) { setError(...); return } before the minN > maxN check. Low priority given browser type=number enforcement, but makes the validation complete.

residual_risks: fetch_ohlcv_async dedup creates shared-fate behavior: all bot coroutines polling the same symbol simultaneously will either all succeed or all fail together on a given tick. This is a behavioral change from the old per-bot run_in_executor pattern. The blast radius on a transient network error is wider.

BatchQuickBacktestRequest.symbols has no maximum length constraint. Individual symbol validation is applied transitively via QuickBacktestRequest construction inside the loop, so per-symbol validation is correct. An oversized list causes prolonged blocking in the thread pool worker.
buy_logic and sell_logic fields in QuickBacktestRequest and BatchQuickBacktestRequest accept arbitrary strings; only AND and OR are handled downstream. Pre-existing, not worsened by this PR.
testing_gaps: No test exercises the fetch_ohlcv_async dedup path: two concurrent coroutines with the same key, verifying that _fetch is called exactly once.
No test verifies that cache_info() returns successfully — would immediately surface the 5-tuple unpack bug as a test failure.
Optimizer validation does not have a test for NaN min/max input (parseFloat of empty string after type=number bypass).
SetFundRequest with amount=0 is valid per the schema (ge=0) but FundBar re-shows the Set Fund prompt when fund.bot_fund === 0 — round-trip behavior of setting fund to 0 is not tested.
